revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏寺]]
'''Clan temple''' '''Ujidera''' (氏寺) '''Ujidera''' (氏寺) refers to {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|qid=Q5393308|lt=Buddhist temples|1=Buddhist temple}} that were established during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} as new sites for religious rituals by powerful {{ill|Clan|en|Clan|qid=Q211503|lt=uji clans|1=Clan}} and royal families, replacing the role of {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|qid=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}}, particularly {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}, as places of ancestral worship. From the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|qid=Q12554|lt=medieval period|1=Middle Ages}} onwards, these temples came to be referred to as {{ill|Bodaiji|en|Bodaiji|qid=Q1084697|lt=bodaiji|1=Bodaiji}}. Representative examples include: - {{ill|Hōryū-ji|en|Hōryū-ji|qid=Q261932|lt=Hōryū-ji|1=Hōryū-ji}} (also known as Ikaruga-dera), established by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} - {{ill|Asuka-dera|en|Asuka-dera|qid=Q779755|lt=Asuka-dera|1=Asuka-dera}}, built by the {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|qid=Q909854|lt=Soga clan|1=Soga clan}} - {{ill|Kōryū-ji|en|Kōryū-ji|qid=Q3076920|lt=Kōryū-ji|1=Kōryū-ji}}, constructed by the {{ill|Hata clan|en|Hata clan|qid=Q987290|lt=Hata clan|1=Hata clan}} In regional areas, it was common for local chieftains to build their own '''ujidera'''. For example: - The {{ill|Sagami Kokubun-ji|en|Sagami Kokubun-ji|qid=Q11582140|lt=Sagami Kokubun-ji|1=Sagami Kokubun-ji}} exhibits architectural styles older than the {{ill|Tempyo style|ja|天平様式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tempyo style|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, featuring a {{ill|Hōryū-ji style temple layout|ja|法隆寺式伽藍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryū-ji style temple layout|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Hakuhō-style|ja|白鳳様式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuhō-style|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} roof tiles. This suggests that the temple may have been converted from a clan temple (ujidera) to a state-sponsored temple (kokubunji). - Excavations at the {{ill|Chiyo Ruins|ja|千代廃寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiyo Ruins|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135550952|1=Chiyo Temple Ruins}} in {{ill|Odawara|en|Odawara|qid=Q267258|lt=Odawara City|1=Odawara}}, Chiyodai, have also uncovered Hakuhō-style tiles, indicating it may have been a clan temple. Many regional chieftains also served as {{ill|gunji|ja|郡司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gunji|qq=}} (district governors). As {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} evolved from being a belief system primarily embraced by the {{ill|imperial family|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial family|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11581068}} and powerful {{ill|Gōzoku|en|Gōzoku|qid=Q84066|lt=aristocrats|1=Gōzoku}}—who practiced it as a means of protecting the state ({{ill|chingo kokka|ja|鎮護国家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chingo kokka|1=chingo kokka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11651185}})—it gradually spread as a faith for personal salvation throughout the aristocracy. By the medieval period, Buddhism had extended its influence to the {{ill|Buke (Japan)|en|Buke (Japan)|qid=Q2927809|lt=samurai class|1=Buke}} and the general populace, leading to changes in the concept and function of ujidera. In addition to temples serving the religious and memorial needs of entire clans, individual families within those clans also began to establish their own ujidera, often referred to as "family temples" (家寺). These temples performed Buddhist rites tailored to the specific needs of the clan or family. Beyond their religious functions, some ujidera became involved in: - Managing {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|qid=Q1550557|lt=shōen estates|1=Manorialism}} to ensure the temple’s economic sustainability - Providing financial support and counsel to their founding clans or families Rather than being merely passive recipients of patronage, ujidera maintained close relationships with their sponsoring families while pursuing self-sufficiency and growth. This development marks a significant shift in the role of ujidera during the medieval period.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ujigami|en|Ujigami|qid=Q767655|lt=Ujigami|1=Ujigami}} (Clan Deity) * {{ill|Ujinchōja|ja|氏長者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujinchōja|1=Clan head|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11547822}} (Clan Leader) * {{ill|Bodaiji|en|Bodaiji|qid=Q1084697|lt=Bodaiji|1=Bodaiji}} (Family Temple) * {{ill|Shiji (private temples)|ja|私寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiji (private temples)|1=Shiji (private temples)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11594690}} (Private Temples) * {{ill|Jōgakuji|ja|定額寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgakuji|1=jōgaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11452272}} (State-supported Temples) * {{ill|Hosshō-ji|en|Hosshō-ji|qid=Q3141015|lt=Hōshō-ji|1=Hosshō-ji}} - Established in the late Heian period by {{ill|Emperor Shirakawa|en|Emperor Shirakawa|qid=Q60949|lt=Emperor Shirakawa|1=Emperor Shirakawa}}, later revered by subsequent emperors. The temple was praised as the "ujidera of the sovereign" by {{ill|Jien|en|Jien|qid=Q715993|lt=Jien|1=Jien}}.
{{translated page|ja|氏寺|version=104558448|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:氏寺]]
